findings.summary: finalize_document_with_error does not wrap API calls in try/except PAPERLESS_CALL_EXCEPTIONS, unlike every sibling function in tags.py (release_processing_tag, remove_stale_queue_tag, get_latest_tags). If the Paperless API is unreachable during error finalization, the exception propagates up through the worker and is caught only by the generic daemon_loop catch-all, producing an opaque 'Work item failed' log instead of a targeted 'Failed to finalize with error tag' message.
evidence: tags.py:116-148 calls client.update_document and client.update_document_metadata without try/except
tags.py:65-78 (remove_stale_queue_tag) wraps same calls in try/except PAPERLESS_CALL_EXCEPTIONS
tags.py:91-113 (release_processing_tag) wraps same calls in try/except PAPERLESS_CALL_EXCEPTIONS
tags.py:38-49 (get_latest_tags) wraps same call in try/except PAPERLESS_CALL_EXCEPTIONS
findings.suggestion: Wrap the two API calls in finalize_document_with_error with try/except PAPERLESS_CALL_EXCEPTIONS and log a specific failure message, consistent with the error handling pattern used by every other function in the same module.

findings.summary: daemon_loop.py line 106 catches a manually enumerated exception tuple (OSError, httpx.HTTPError, RuntimeError, ValueError, TypeError, KeyError) instead of referencing the centralized PAPERLESS_CALL_EXCEPTIONS constant. This tuple is a superset that also adds RuntimeError and TypeError, creating a parallel exception policy that can drift independently from the canonical definition in paperless.py.
evidence: daemon_loop.py:106 catches (OSError, httpx.HTTPError, RuntimeError, ValueError, TypeError, KeyError)
paperless.py:22 defines PAPERLESS_CALL_EXCEPTIONS = (OSError, httpx.HTTPError, ValueError, KeyError)
Every other call site in the codebase uses PAPERLESS_CALL_EXCEPTIONS by reference
findings.suggestion: Define a named constant (e.g., DAEMON_LOOP_EXCEPTIONS) that composes PAPERLESS_CALL_EXCEPTIONS with the additional types, or add a comment explaining why the superset is intentional. This makes the relationship explicit and prevents silent drift.

findings.summary: Worker unit tests (test_worker.py for both OCR and classifier) patch 4-6 internal module references per test (bytes_to_images, assemble_full_text, claim_processing_tag, release_processing_tag, etc.) and assert on mock call counts and argument shapes. These tests are tightly coupled to implementation wiring rather than observable behavior, meaning any internal refactor (e.g., inlining a helper or changing call order) breaks tests even when behavior is unchanged.
evidence: test_worker.py:40-43 patches four module-level references for a single test
Tests assert mock_claim.assert_called_once(), mock_release.assert_called_once() etc. which test wiring not behavior
findings.suggestion: Consider adding a few higher-level worker tests that inject fake collaborators (rather than patching imports) and assert on observable outcomes (e.g., what metadata was sent to paperless_client) rather than internal call sequences. The existing integration tests partially address this but don't cover error paths.

findings.summary: 949 occurrences of '# Arrange', '# Act', '# Assert' comments appear across 16 test files. While the AAA pattern is sound, these comments are mechanical markers that restate the obvious structure of every test without adding insight. They are a hallmark of AI-generated test code and add visual noise.
evidence: test_tags.py uses the pattern on trivial 3-line tests like test_returns_set_of_ints_from_doc_tags
test_claims.py uses it on every single test method without exception
findings.suggestion: Remove the AAA comments. The test structure is already clear from the code itself. If a test is complex enough to need section markers, it may benefit from extraction into helper methods instead.

findings.summary: Several short functions have docstrings that restate the function name and signature without adding domain insight. Examples: 'Return ``True`` if the image is essentially blank (all white).' on is_blank, 'Return how many documents reference this taxonomy item.' on _get_usage_count, 'Deduplicate a model list while preserving insertion order.' on unique_models (a one-liner).
evidence: llm.py:89-91 unique_models has a docstring for a single-line `list(dict.fromkeys(models))`

taxonomy.py:60-78 _get_usage_count docstring restates 'Return how many documents reference this taxonomy item' but the Paperless version-compat logic in the body is the non-obvious part that deserves explanation
findings.suggestion: For trivial functions, either omit the docstring or use it to explain WHY (e.g., version compatibility reasons) rather than restating WHAT the code already shows.
